advantages: Tests run against the installed package — the same code path users get.
No ambient imports from cwd; imports are explicit and reproducible.
Forces you to keep `pyproject.toml` honest about dependencies and packages.
Tooling (pytest, mypy, ruff) discovers the package via installed metadata, fewer 'works on my machine' surprises.
Clean separation between *what ships* (`src/`) and *what supports shipping* (tests, scripts, docs, CI).
disadvantages: Requires `pip install -e .` before you can `python -c 'import yourpkg'` from the repo root.
Extra step to onboard contributors who expect flat layouts.
For tiny single-file utilities, the overhead isn't worth it.
Some legacy tools and tutorials assume flat layout; you'll occasionally translate.
Slightly more deep paths in your repo (`src/pkg/sub/...`).
